html:
Excelliance MOS Corp. is not the cash-rich governance unwind described in the prior drafts. That version overstated 2025 earnings and cash by a lot. The real thesis is cleaner and still interesting: this is a tiny Taiwan power MOSFET and power-management IC designer whose earnings base is depressed, whose gross margin is already proving better than the revenue line, and whose large liquid-asset portfolio makes the screen-level P/E less informative than forward earnings power.

At TWD 99.40, 5299.TWO has a market cap of about TWD 5.1bn, or roughly USD 160m. The company’s own profile says it designs and sells power components and power management ICs for notebooks, motherboards, power supplies, home appliances, power tools, BMS, LCD/TV and related power-conversion use cases. The stock is small, lightly institutionalized and very retail-owned: Goodinfo shows only about 1.4% foreign ownership in the 2025 holder mix, while domestic individuals and domestic corporates each hold roughly 48%. HiStock shows only 349 followers and local retail discussion still framed around trading losses and whether to stop out. This is not a globally owned AI supply-chain name.

The bear case is obvious on the surface. 2025 revenue was TWD 1.48bn, down 12.3%, with Q4 revenue only TWD 307m. Full-year 2025 EPS was only TWD 4.58, so the stock at TWD 99.40 screens near 21x trailing earnings. That is why the stock can be dismissed quickly.

The variant view is that 2025 is the wrong earnings base. Q1 2026 revenue was still down 4.8% year over year, but EPS rose to TWD 1.65 from TWD 1.39. Gross margin expanded to 34.5% from 32.9%, and net margin reached 21.8%. April revenue of TWD 143.6m was almost flat year over year, leaving Jan-April revenue down only 3.6%. The revenue line has not inflected hard yet, but the margin line has. For a small fabless power IC business with fixed R&D and admin costs, that is the part that matters.

The key misunderstanding is that the company is not just a cyclical chip stock with a weak 2025. It is a depressed-revenue operating company sitting on a material financial asset base. At September 2025 it had TWD 273m of cash, TWD 1.06bn of longer-duration time deposits and repo-like financial assets, and TWD 164m of current fair-value financial assets. Long-term borrowings were essentially immaterial. In May 2026 it also announced a TWD 100m purchase of Fubon Life subordinated bonds using its own funds, bringing that specific bond holding to TWD 170m. This balance sheet produces real interest income, but it also confuses the screen because quarterly earnings include finance gains and losses. Q2 2025, for example, had positive operating income but negative EPS because of non-operating losses. A trailing P/E screen misses the current-cycle operating recovery.

The business itself is not glamorous, which is the opportunity. Excelliance makes the kinds of power parts that sit inside PCs, power supplies and increasingly broader power-conversion applications. Management has been explicit that it wants more integrated products and more energy-saving power-management use cases. The first evidence is not a hockey-stick revenue guide; it is that gross margin is holding in the mid-30s even while revenue is only stabilizing. If revenue returns merely to the 2024 level of about TWD 1.69bn, with Q1 2026 margin structure intact, EPS can move from the TWD 4.6 trailing area toward TWD 7-plus without heroic assumptions.

The capital return is useful, not decisive. The board declared a TWD 3.66 cash dividend for 2025, with ex-dividend trading scheduled for July 14, 2026. That is a 3.7% yield at TWD 99.40. It is not the 8% yield imagined in the prior draft, but it does pay investors to wait while the revenue base normalizes.

The setup is best understood as a forward operating leverage trade with asset backing. At the current price, the stock trades around 1.6x book, roughly 15x Q1 annualized EPS, and closer to the low-teens on a normalized 2026-2027 EPS base if revenue recovers toward 2024 levels and Q1 gross margin persists. That is not dirt-cheap on stale numbers. It is cheap if the market is looking backward at 2025 while the company is already earning at a materially better run rate.

The risk is that Q1 was a financial-income-assisted quarter and revenue never actually turns. If PC and power-supply demand stay weak, gross margin can slip, operating leverage works in reverse, and the stock becomes a 1.2x book small-cap semiconductor value trap. The other risk is capital allocation: fixed-income investments are fine, but investors would prefer cash returns or high-ROI product development over a quasi-treasury portfolio. Liquidity is also thin enough that position sizing matters.

The base case is simple. Revenue returns toward TWD 1.65bn to TWD 1.75bn over the next year, gross margin holds around 34%, non-operating income normalizes, and EPS reaches roughly TWD 7.0 to TWD 7.5. Put an 18x multiple on that because the earnings recovery is visible and the balance sheet is strong, and the stock is worth about TWD 130.

The bull case is that AI PC, Windows refresh, replacement demand, and broader power-conversion sockets lift revenue back above TWD 2.0bn while gross margin stays in the mid-30s. EPS can approach TWD 8.5 to TWD 9.0, and a small Taiwan power IC name with improving growth can trade near 19x. That gives a TWD 170 stock.

The bear case is TWD 75: revenue stagnates near 2025 levels, gross margin fades, EPS falls back toward TWD 4, and the market values the company at about 1.2x book despite the dividend and liquid assets.

Current stock price: TWD 99.40. Bear target TWD 75, down 25%. Base target TWD 130, up 31%. Bull target TWD 170, up 71%.
